dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding to a falls call at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My wrist is really hurting and looks deformed. I fell about 30 minutes ago.
Ma'am, can you look at me and tell me your name?
I'm Jane.
Okay, Jane. Where are we now?
I'm in [CITY]. I fell in my home.
Is there anything blocking your airway or are you having difficulty breathing?
No, I can breathe fine. I'm sitting upright.
Respirations are at 20 per minute.
Your breathing looks normal, and you're speaking without apparent difficulty. Do you have chest pain or trouble with that?
Radial pulses are strong, equal, and regular. 13. Primary Assessment; Skin appearance: Color; EMT: You have normal skin color. Are you noticing any paleness?
No, it feels warm to me. I'm not feeling any numbness.
Pulse rate is 89 and regular.
Blood pressure is 136 over 79.
We've placed you on the EKG monitor. It's showing sinus rhythm.
Jane, can you explain what happened and what symptoms you're having now?

I was walking to get water, tripped, and fell on my right hand. My wrist looks deformed and is in a lot of pain. I'm diabetic and take insulin, but today my blood sugar was high. I've also got high blood pressure, osteoarthritis, had a stroke before with an infarction, and experience seizures from an old clot.
That includes a stroke from a blood clot or infarction, correct? And hyperlipidemia as well?
Yeah, that's right. High cholesterol is part of my medical issues too.
When exactly did the pain begin?
It began when I fell.
Would you describe it as sharp, crushing, burning, or something else?
It's a 10 out of 10.
How long ago did it start?
It's just in my wrist, no other part of my body.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - mental status / short & long term memory assessment
dialogue.utterance: What's today's date and where are you now?

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - mental status / short & long term memory assessment
dialogue.utterance: I don't know the exact date, but I'm at home in [CITY].
Your skin is normal in color with no visible bruising or redness.
The skin is warm and dry.
You're fully alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation with a GCS of 15.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Can you feel my touch and move your fingers as I ask?
Yes, I can feel your touch. My wrist is just misshapen and bent in an odd way.
Right wrist is clearly deformed and tender to the touch.
Pupils are 3-mm bilaterally and equal.
You're fully alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (AOx4).
Pulse is 89 beats per minute and regular.
Respirations are 20 per minute.
Blood pressure is 136 over 79.
SpO2 is 99 percent on room air.
EKG strips show sinus rhythm.
Blood glucose is 595 mg/dL.
We're going to administer fentanyl intranasally for your pain. That's part of our standing orders.
We've attached the end-tidal CO2 monitor to track your breathing during medication administration.
We've given you fentanyl via the nostrils. Let's watch for improvement.
Let's attempt an IV in your left hand first.
First site infiltrated. Trying a second site now.
Still no success placing the IV. We'll hold off for now.
Applying a splint with a cold pack to reduce swelling and immobilize the wrist.
Splint and cold pack added. Deforming movement should be limited with this configuration.
Placed you on the 4-lead EKG as part of protocol.
Placing you in a comfortable position with a sling for your arm-this is based on your report of shoulder discomfort.
Spo2 is still at 99 percent.
Is your pain feeling better?
Yes, it's now about 7. Thank you for that.
Following the pain control and general trauma management protocols.
We can transport you to [COMPANY_NAME]. That's your preferred destination, correct?
Yes, that works for me.
I'll lift you gently under the arms now. Don't worry, I'll keep your arm supported.
Transporting you to the ambulance and securing you for movement.
Transferring you to ED18 using the pull sheet method with assistance from on-site nursing staff.
Transfer complete. Receiving nurse has accepted all care and has briefed on patient findings.
Administering a second dose of fentanyl per protocol and standing order. You responded well last time, so this should help further.
Fentanyl second dose given. You indicated you felt improvement, went down to a 5.
Pulse is now at 90 per minute and regular.
Respirations are at 17 per minute.
Blood pressure updated and noted as 146 over 82.
Continued sinus rhythm on EKG monitoring.
Spo2 is still fine at 99 percent.
Your breathing remains normal and high oxygen levels. Given your history of seizures and past stroke, we'll continue monitoring you during transport.
I feel better now, as long as it doesn't get worse.
No worries, we're monitoring you the whole way.
Skin is still warm and non-pale. Used a general splint for wrist injury, keeping it immobilized and in place.
Dispatch, Unit 3 en route with a trauma patient for right wrist injury and high blood glucose.
